title: The Topography Trap: Sifting Interlayer Excitons from Strain-Related Artifacts in Real-World 2D Hetrostructures
authors: Pablo Hernández López, Luka Pirker, Astrid Weston, Arijit Kayal, Rafael Nadas, Adrián Dewambrechies Fernández, Álvaro Rodríguez, Roman Gorbachev, Kirill I. Bolotin, Otakar Frank, Sebastian Heeg
abstract: Novel excitonic phenomena emerging in transition metal dichalcogenide (TMDC) heterostructures belong to the most exciting topics in contemporary physics of van der Waals materials. Interlayer excitons (IXs) stand out among those due to their long radiative lifetimes and tunability by electric fields, strain, and twist angle. However, many ambiguities persist in the optical identification and manipulation of IXs, highlighting the need for reliable spectroscopic criteria that distinguish interlayer species from spurious signals. Here, we present a decision-tree protocol that evaluates interlayer coupling via intralayer exciton quenching and correlates photoluminescence (PL) with atomic force microscopy (AFM) to correctly assign room-temperature PL features in TMDC-based heterostructures. Applying this protocol, we identify momentum-direct IX between the K valleys of the two layers (KK-IX) in MoS2-MoSe2 and MoS2-WSe2 heterostructures at room temperature. In contrast, our protocol contests the reported bright, momentum-indirect, twist-angle-independent $Γ$K-IX in MoS2-WSe2. Comprehensive experimental data, including infrared and tip-enhanced photoluminescence (TEPL) with sub-diffraction-limited resolution, show no compelling evidence for this excitonic species, despite numerous reports. Instead, the spectroscopic features previously assigned to this $Γ$K-IX originate from locally strained WSe2 at topographical inhomogeneities of the heterostructure interface, underscoring the need for robust, spatially resolved characterization of real-world samples in this highly accessible field and providing a generally applicable framework for identifying interlayer excitons in 2D semiconductor heterostructures.
summary: This paper introduces a systematic protocol to distinguish true interlayer excitons from strain-induced artifacts in 2D heterostructures. By correlating spectroscopy with topography, the authors prove that certain widely reported excitonic species are actually signals from strained monolayers trapped in interface bubbles. This work provides a much-needed framework for reliable spectroscopic identification in the field of van der Waals materials.
main_problem: The difficulty in distinguishing genuine interlayer excitons (IXs) from spurious signals caused by strain and topographical defects (bubbles/wrinkles) in 2D TMDC heterostructures.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that previously reported bright, momentum-indirect GammaK-IX in MoS2-WSe2 is actually a strain-induced artifact of WSe2 intralayer excitons, and they provide a decision-tree protocol to prevent such misidentifications.
method: A three-step decision-tree protocol involving intralayer exciton quenching analysis, energy thresholding, and spatial correlation between photoluminescence (PL) and AFM topography, supplemented by Tip-Enhanced PL (TEPL).
model_or_system: 2D transition metal dichalcogenide (TMDC) heterostructures, specifically MoS2-MoSe2 and MoS2-WSe2, featuring Type-II band alignment and topographical irregularities like bubbles and wrinkles.
key_observables: Photoluminescence (PL) intensity, emission energy, intralayer exciton quenching factor, AFM topography, and sub-diffraction-limited TEPL maps.
important_parameters: Twist angle, energy separation between peaks (threshold of 200 meV), quenching percentage (threshold of 90%), and strain levels (0.2-0.7%).
main_assumptions: The protocol assumes that high quenching of intralayer excitons is a reliable proxy for efficient interlayer charge transfer.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the decision-tree protocol flowchart; Figure 2 demonstrates the application of the protocol to MoS2-MoSe2 and the identification of strain artifacts in MoS2-WSe2 via PL/AFM correlation; Figure 3 uses TEPL to resolve the nanoscale origin of the strain-induced signal.
paper_structure: Introduction of the identification problem, presentation of the three-step decision-tree protocol, experimental validation using MoS2-MoSe2, critical analysis/refutation of the GammaK-IX in MoS2-WSe2 using AFM and TEPL, and conclusion on the necessity of robust characterization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
